rows.title: CDMG
rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[l_quotient_vs_ordered_pair_typing_inconsistent] The set $L$ of bidirected edges may be encoded either as a subset of the quotient $V \times V / {\sim}$ (with $(v_1, v_2) \sim (v_2, v_1)$, i.e., as unordered pairs) or as a subset of $V \times V$ subject to the symmetry constraint $(v_1, v_2) \in L \iff (v_2, v_1) \in L$. Both encodings are admissible; in the ordered-pair encoding, membership of $(v_1, v_2)$ and $(v_2, v_1)$ must always coincide. The irreflexivity condition $v_1 \neq v_2$ applies under either encoding.

[edge_set_disjointness_under_specified] The qualifier "disjoint" applied to the edge sets $E$ and $L$ is to be read as trivial type-level disjointness — directed edges and bidirected edges are objects of distinct types — and imposes no graph-theoretic mutual-exclusion constraint between them. In particular, for $v_1, v_2 \in V$, it is permitted to have $(v_1, v_2) \in E$ and $\{v_1, v_2\} \in L$ simultaneously; if a formalization encodes both $E$ and $L$ as subsets of ordered pairs over $V$, the same ordered pair $(v, w)$ may belong to both $E$ and $L$.

[manual_1] Node sets J and V are both finite.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: bidirected_edge_quotient_vs_implication_redundant
explanation: The set $L$ is defined as a subset of the quotient $V \times V / ((v_1,v_2) \sim (v_2,v_1))$, i.e., elements of $L$ are equivalence classes of ordered pairs identified with their reverses. Under this reading, the equivalence class of $(v_1, v_2)$ literally *is* the equivalence class of $(v_2, v_1)$, so "$(v_2, v_1) \in L$" is automatic whenever "$(v_1, v_2) \in L$".

But the constraint then states: "$(v_1,v_2) \in L \implies v_1 \neq v_2 \land (v_2,v_1) \in L$". The conjunct "$(v_2,v_1) \in L$" is redundant under the quotient interpretation — it's already forced by the very definition of $L$.

This signals one of two things:
1. The author intended $L$ as a set of *ordered* pairs (not equivalence classes) and the symmetry "$(v_2, v_1) \in L$" is the real constraint, in which case the quotient notation is misleading.
2. The author intended $L$ as equivalence classes, in which case the symmetry part of the implication is vacuous and only "$v_1 \neq v_2$" carries content.

The literal text is internally inconsistent in whether elements of $L$ are ordered or unordered. A formalizer must pick one; the choice will leak into every downstream definition that quantifies "for each bidirected edge $(v_1,v_2) \in L$" (does the same edge then also appear as $(v_2, v_1)$, or is each undirected pair encountered exactly once?).
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: edge_sets_E_and_L_disjointness_ill_typed
explanation: The text says "two \emph{(disjoint)} sets of edges" and then defines:
- $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ — a set of ordered pairs.
- $L \subseteq V \times V / ((v_1,v_2) \sim (v_2,v_1))$ — a set of equivalence classes.

These live in different ambient sets, so on the most literal reading the disjointness condition is either trivially satisfied (a raw ordered pair is never equal to an equivalence class of pairs, so they cannot share an element) or simply ill-typed.

But the author plainly meant *something* by writing "(disjoint)". A plausible intended reading is: for any $v_1, v_2 \in V$, you cannot have both a directed edge $(v_1, v_2) \in E$ (or $(v_2, v_1) \in E$) and a bidirected edge $\{v_1, v_2\} \in L$ between the same two vertices. This is a much stronger and more interesting structural constraint, and standard ADMG-style mixed graphs sometimes do/don't enforce it depending on the author.

The literal text does not pin this down. A formalizer who takes the strict type-disjointness reading will produce a CDMG type where the same vertex pair can carry both kinds of edge; one who takes the "no parallel directed+bidirected edge between same pair" reading will produce a different (and stricter) type. Downstream proofs about, e.g., edge counts or path structure will behave differently.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: directed_self_loops_unrestricted_in_E
explanation: The definition of the directed edge set is simply "$E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ the set of directed edges", with no exclusion of the diagonal. For any $v \in V$, the pair $(v, v)$ lies in $(J \cup V) \times V$, so the literal definition admits $(v, v) \in E$ — a directed self-loop at $v$.

By contrast, bidirected edges *do* explicitly forbid self-loops via "$v_1 \neq v_2$".

This asymmetry — directed self-loops permitted, bidirected self-loops forbidden — is unannotated in the text. In the standard ADMG/CDMG literature directed self-loops are usually excluded (since they trivially break acyclicity and complicate notions like parents/ancestors). If the author intended that exclusion, it has been silently omitted. A literal formalization will allow $v \to v$, which is likely to surface as an unexpected corner case in any later definition that recurses over directed edges (paths, ancestral sets, topological orderings, d-separation).
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: empty_vertex_set_admitted
explanation: The active text places no nonemptiness condition on the vertex sets. The literal definition therefore admits $J = V = \emptyset$ (forcing also $E = L = \emptyset$): the "empty CDMG" is a legal CDMG. Whether this degenerate object is intended to be a legitimate inhabitant of the type is unclear from the tex alone. Downstream constructions that, e.g., pick a vertex, take a marginal over a non-empty subset, or assert the existence of a sink/source vertex, will need to either add a hypothesis at the use site or rule out the empty case here. The literal reading leaves this ambiguous.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.title: CDMGNotation
rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[sus_omits_tail_tail_despite_star_eq_head_or_tail] CDMGs do not admit a tail-tail edge type; consequently the placeholder rule "the star stands for arrowhead or tail" yields only the three enumerated configurations in item 7 (the tail-tail case "$v_1 \tut v_2$" is excluded by definition, not omitted by oversight). Any case analysis on "$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$" may assume exhaustiveness over exactly the three listed alternatives $\{\tuh, \hut, \huh\}$.

[huh_visual_symmetry_vs_ordered_pair_in_L] The relation $L$ is required to be symmetric: for all $v_1, v_2$, $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ if and only if $(v_2, v_1) \in L$. Consequently, $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \iff v_2 \huh v_1 \in G$, and the symmetry of the $\huh$-disjunct propagates to items 5–7 accordingly.

wording_check.output:
I'll audit only the active (uncommented) tex content of def_3_2's block. Two subtleties surfaced.

```
SUBTLETY:
id: star_placeholder_omits_tail_tail_case_in_sus
explanation: The trailing sentence states: "The star stands for a placeholder to mean: ``arrowhead or tail''." Applied mechanically to the compound symbol \sus ("star, star"), this rule combinatorially generates four cases for the marks at the two endpoints:
  - tail/tail = \tut (an undirected edge)
  - tail/head = \tuh (a forward directed edge)
  - head/tail = \hut (a backward directed edge)
  - head/head = \huh (a bidirected edge)

But the definition of \sus only enumerates three: "$v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$". The tail/tail case "$v_1 \tut v_2$" is silently excluded. The text neither names \tut, nor explains the asymmetry between the placeholder rule (which is general) and the \sus enumeration (which is incomplete with respect to that rule).

The presumed reason is that CDMGs have no undirected edges, so \tut has no representation in $G$ — but def_3_2 itself does not say so. By contrast, the placeholder rule produces exactly the right number of cases for the two two-element compounds: \suh expands to {\tuh, \huh} (both listed) and \hus expands to {\hut, \huh} (both listed). Only \sus has a "missing" combinatorial case.

A literal reader applying the placeholder rule to predict what \sus means may either (i) be surprised by the omission, or (ii) think they're free to extend \sus to also include \tut as a vacuously-false disjunct. A formalizer doing case analysis on "$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$" needs an explicit guarantee that exhaustiveness is over {\tuh, \hut, \huh} and not over the full {\tut, \tuh, \hut, \huh}.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: huh_membership_writes_ordered_pair_and_lacks_local_symmetry
explanation: Item 4 says: "$v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1, v_2) \in L$." Two distinct subtleties arise from this single sentence.

(a) Type/encoding presupposition. The block opens with "Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG", inheriting whatever ambient type def_3_1 chose for $L$. Def_3_1's literal text declares $L \subseteq V \times V / ((v_1,v_2) \sim (v_2,v_1))$, i.e., as a subset of the quotient by edge-reversal — so on the strictest reading, elements of $L$ are equivalence classes, not ordered pairs. Under that reading, def_3_2's phrasing "$(v_1, v_2) \in L$" is literally type-mismatched: an ordered pair is being asserted as an element of a set of equivalence classes. The intended meaning is presumably "$[(v_1, v_2)]_\sim \in L$", but def_3_2 commits, by its choice of notation, to the alternative ordered-pair encoding of $L$ — without flagging that this is a choice.

(b) Symmetry of \huh is not stated within def_3_2. The definition gives $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \iff (v_1, v_2) \in L$ and would give $v_2 \huh v_1 \in G \iff (v_2, v_1) \in L$. As ordered pairs these are different, so a reader who restricts attention to def_3_2 alone would not be able to conclude $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \iff v_2 \huh v_1 \in G$. The equivalence holds only because def_3_1 separately imposes the symmetry constraint "$(v_1, v_2) \in L \implies (v_2, v_1) \in L$" (the same constraint that is, under the quotient encoding mentioned in (a), already automatic). The notation block neither restates this constraint nor remarks that \huh is symmetric.

This propagates: items 5–7 (\suh, \hus, \sus) all use \huh as a disjunct, so any downstream argument that, e.g., treats "$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$" as a property of the unordered pair $\{v_1, v_2\}$ tacitly invokes the external symmetry constraint to handle the \huh disjunct. A reader who never opens def_3_1 cannot verify this from def_3_2's text.
END_SUBTLETY
```

rows.title: EdgeRelations
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [inconsistent_writing_order_enumeration_into_vs_out_of] Throughout the enumeration above, the writings $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ denote the same edge (and the bidirected writings $v_1 \huh v_2$ and $v_2 \huh v_1$ likewise coincide). Membership of an edge in the categories "into $v$" or "out of $v$" depends only on the underlying element of $E$, not on which of these textual writings is used; item 3's explicit listing of both writings and item 2's single-writing enumeration are therefore equivalent under this identification.

rows.title: CDMGRestrictions
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [implicit_universal_quantifier_in_hus_clause] The statements "$j \hus v \notin G$" and "edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed" carry implicit universal quantification over $v$. Precisely: for every $j \in J$ and every node $v$ in the graph, $j \hus v \notin G$; and for every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, an edge $j \tuh v$ is permitted in $G$ (its presence is not asserted, only that no restriction forbids it).

rows.title: Walks
rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Walks]\label{def:walks}
     Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $v,w \in G$.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item A \emph{walk} from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a finite alternating sequence of adjacent nodes and edges 
      \[v=v_0, a_0,  v_1, \dots v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n=w\] 
%      \[v=v_0 \sus v_1 \sus  \cdots \sus v_{n-1} \sus v_n=w\]
        in $G$ for some $n \ge 0$, i.e.\ such that for every $k=0,\dots,n-1$ 
        we have that $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \cup L$ or $a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$, and with end nodes $v_0=v$ and $v_n=w$.
        An example walk from $v_0$ to $v_3$ could look like:
        \[v_0 \tuh v_1 \hut  v_2 \huh v_3, \qquad\text{ with } \qquad  v_0 \tuh v_1, v_2 \tuh v_1 \in E,\,v_2 \huh v_3 \in L. \]
        The same node may appear multiple times in a walk.
        Also the \emph{trivial walk} consisting of a single node $v_0 \in G$ is allowed (if $v=w$).
        The walk is called \emph{into $v_0$} if $a_0 = v_0 \hus v_1$, and \emph{out of $v_0$} if $a_0 = v_0 \tuh v_1$.
        Similarly, it is called \emph{into $v_n$} if $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \suh v_n$ and \emph{out of $v_n$} if $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \hut v_n$.
    \item A \emph{directed walk} from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form:
        \[v=v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh  \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w,\]
        for some $n \ge 0$,
        where all arrowheads point in the direction of $w$ and there are no arrowheads pointing back.
   % \item[] Directed walks exclude the trivial walk per definition.
    \item A \emph{bidirected walk} from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form:
        \[v=v_0 \huh v_1 \huh  \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh v_n=w,\]
        for some $n \ge 0$, where all edges are bidirected.
    \item A \emph{collider walk} from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form:
        \[v=v_0 \suh v_1 \huh  \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus v_n=w,\]
        for some $n \ge 0$, where all nodes in between $v$ and $w$ have two arrowheads pointing towards them (a.k.a.\ collider).
        Note that for $n=1$ this reads: $v\sus w \in G$.
    \item A walk is called \emph{path} if no node occurs more than once.
    \item A \emph{bifurcation} between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ is a walk of the form:
        \[v=v_0 \hut v_1 \hut  \cdots \hut v_{k-1} \hus v_k  \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w,\]
   %             \[v=v_0 \hut v_1 \hut  \cdots \hut v_{k-1} \huh v_k  \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w,\]
        such that $v \ne w$, the walk contains both endnodes exactly once, 
	the subwalk $v_0 \hut \cdots v_{k-1}$ is a directed walk from $v_{k-1}$ to $v_0$,
	and the subwalk $v_k \tuh \cdots v_n$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_n$.
        %every node has at most one arrowhead pointing towards it, and 
        %both endnodes have exactly one arrowhead pointing towards them. 
        If the edge $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$ is directed ($v_{k-1} \hut v_k$) then we say that the bifurcation has \emph{source} $v_{k}$.
  \end{enumerate}
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}
rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[bifurcation_right_chain_trivial_is_just_directed_walk] As an additional constraint on the bifurcation form, both endnodes $v_0$ and $v_n$ must have exactly one arrowhead pointing towards them. In particular, this excludes the degenerate case $k = n$ with directed hinge $v_{n-1} \hut v_n$, in which the walk reduces to a directed walk $v_0 \leftarrow v_1 \leftarrow \cdots \leftarrow v_n$ and $v_n$ has no arrowhead pointing towards it.

[collider_walk_n1_form_contradicts_inline_note] For $n=1$, a collider walk from $v$ to $w$ requires a bidirected edge $v \huh w \in E$ (arrowheads at both endpoints), as dictated by the form $v_0 \suh v_1$ combined with $v_{n-1} \hus v_n$ collapsing onto the single edge. The inline note "$v \sus w \in G$" is to be read in this stricter sense and does *not* admit purely directed edges $v \tuh w$ or $v \hut w$ as collider walks of length 1.

rows.title: FamilyRelationships
rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Family relationships]
     Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $v,w \in V$ and $A \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes.
     We then define:
     \begin{enumerate}
         \item The set of \emph{parents} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, w \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
         \item[] The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).\]
         %\item[] \PF{maybe better for sets (???) :The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \lp \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v)\rp \sm A.\]
         %    but then  if $v \tuh v \in E$ then $v \notin \Pa^G(\lC v\rC ) \neq \Pa^G(v) \ni v$ ... }
         \item The set of \emph{children} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
         \item[] The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
         \item The set of \emph{siblings} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Sib^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \huh w \in G \rC.\]
         %\item[] The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
         \item The set of \emph{ancestors} of $v$ in $G$: 
             \[\Anc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } w \tuh \cdots \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The set of \emph{ancestors} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$.
         \item The set of \emph{descendants} of $v$ in $G$: 
             \[\Desc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } v \tuh \cdots \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v \in \Desc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The set of \emph{descendants} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$.
         \item The set of \emph{non-descendants} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).\]
         \item The \emph{strongly connected component} of $v$ in $G$:
             \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).\]
             Note: $v \in \Sc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The (union of) \emph{strongly connected components} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$.
         \item The \emph{district} of $v$ in $G$:
             \[\Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in G\,|\, \exists \text{ bidirected walk: } 
             v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v\in \Dist^G(v)$.
         \item[] The \emph{district} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[\Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$.
         %\item The d-\emph{Markov blanket} of $v$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_d(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ collider walk: } 
         %    v \suh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus w \in G \rC \sm \{v\}.\]
         %    Note: $\lp\Pa^G(v) \cup \Ch^G(v) \cup \Pa^G(\Ch^G(v))\rp \sm \{v\} \ins \MBl^G_d(v)$.
         %\item[] The d-\emph{Markov blanket} of $A$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_d(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \MBl^G_d(v) \sm A.\]
         %\item The $\sigma$-\emph{Markov blanket} of $A$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_\sigma(A):= \MBl^{G^\acy}_d(A), \]
         %    where $G^\acy$ is an acyclification of $G$, a notion defined below in Definition~\ref{def:cdg_acyclification}.
     \end{enumerate}
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}
rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[self_membership_notes_require_length_zero_walks] In the definitions of $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, and $\Dist^G(v)$, the directed and bidirected walks are understood to include the trivial walk of length $0$ consisting of the single vertex $v$ with no edges. Consequently, $v \in \Anc^G(v)$, $v \in \Desc^G(v)$, $v \in \Sc^G(v)$, and $v \in \Dist^G(v)$ hold for every $v \in V$ unconditionally, regardless of whether any edge (in particular any self-loop) incident to $v$ exists in $G$.

[type_mismatch_individual_vs_set_versions] The preamble should read "$v, w \in J \cup V$" rather than "$v, w \in V$": the individual definitions $\Pa^G(v)$, $\Ch^G(v)$, $\Sib^G(v)$, $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, and $\Dist^G(v)$ are defined for any $v \in J \cup V$, using the same set-builder expressions given. In particular, input nodes ($v \in J$) may have children, descendants, etc., and the set-version definitions $\Pa^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v)$ (and analogously for the other operators) are well-typed for $A \ins J \cup V$ because each summand is defined.

[district_walk_indexing_ambiguous_for_small_n] $w \in \Dist^G(v)$ iff there exists a walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ in which every edge is bidirected ($\huh$). The walk may have any length $\geq 0$; in particular, the length-$0$ walk at $v$ witnesses $v \in \Dist^G(v)$, so neither a bidirected self-loop at $v$ nor a length-$2$ bidirected cycle through $v$ is required. The intermediate-node indexing $v_1, \ldots, v_{n-1}$ in the displayed formula should be read as syntactic sugar for "walk consisting of bidirected edges" and imposes no lower bound on walk length.

rows.title: Acyclicity
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [nontrivial_directed_walk_not_defined_in_block] A non-trivial directed walk is a directed walk $v_0 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n$ with $n \ge 1$ (i.e., one that traverses at least one edge); the trivial walk consisting of a single node $v_0$ is excluded. In particular, a self-loop $v \tuh v$ constitutes a non-trivial directed walk from $v$ to itself, so an acyclic CDMG contains no self-loops on any $v \in V$.

TopologicalOrder

AcyclicPreservedUnderDo
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [w_is_free_unquantified_in_claim_body] The variable $W$ is universally quantified, ranging over all $W$ for which $G_{\doit(W)}$ is defined per the preceding definition of the $\doit$ operator; any side condition on $W$ (such as $W \subseteq V(G)$) is inherited from that definition rather than restated here.

rows.title: HardInterventionsCommute
BifurcationAlternative
NodeSplittingOn
rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Node-splitting on CDMGs]
  \label{def:G_node-splitting}
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W \ins V$ a subset of the output nodes.
    The \emph{node-split graph} w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CDMG: 
    \[ G_{\spl(W)} :=\lp J_{\spl(W)}, V_{\spl(W)}, E_{\spl(W)},L_{\spl(W)} \rp,\]
    %\[ G_{\spl(W)} := G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    %\[G((V\sm W) \dcup W^o|\doit(W^i \dcup J)):=G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    constructed as follows.
    We first make two disjont copies of the nodes in $W$:
    \[ W^0:=\lC w^0\st w \in W \rC, \qquad W^1:=\lC w^1 \st w \in W \rC.  \]
    Note that we consider $w^0 \neq w^1$ for $w \in W$.
    Additionally (for convenience), for $v \in J \cup V \sm W$ we put:
  \[ v^0:=v^1:=v.  \]
  We then define:
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
      \item $J_{\spl(W)} := J$,
      \item $V_{\spl(W)} := (V \sm W) \dcup W^0 \dcup W^1$, %which could be identified with $V$ again if we want to make the identification $W=W^0$.
      \item $E_{\spl(W)} := \lC v^1_1 \tuh v_2^0 \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \cup \lC w^0 \tuh w^1 \st w \in W \rC$,
      \item $L_{\spl(W)} :=\lC v_1^0 \huh v_2^0 \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$.
  \end{enumerate}
  So all incoming edges onto nodes in $W$ become incoming edges into the corresponding nodes in $W^0$,
  all outgoing edges out of nodes in $W$ become outgoing edges out of the corresponding nodes in $W^1$,
  and edges $w^0 \tuh w^1$ are added for all nodes in $W$.
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [disjointness_of_new_copies_only_partially_stipulated] The disjointness assertions $W^0 \cap V = W^1 \cap V = W^0 \cap J = W^1 \cap J = W^0 \cap W^1 = \emptyset$ are to be realized at the type level: in a formal encoding, $W^0$ and $W^1$ are constructed as tagged copies (e.g. via a $\Sigma$-type or tagged sum) so that their elements are of a distinct type from elements of $V$ and $J$, making disjointness hold automatically rather than as a side condition. The convention $v^0 := v^1 := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ is purely notational shorthand used inside the definitions of $E_{\spl(W)}$ and $L_{\spl(W)}$, and does not reassign these elements to the tagged copy types.

TwoDisjointNode
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [cdmg_vs_cadmg_terminology_mismatch] The term "CADMG" appearing in the statement is a typographical inconsistency and should be read as "CDMG". The node-splitting operation $\spl(\cdot)$ takes a CDMG to a CDMG, so both iterated splittings $\lp G_{\spl(W_1)} \rp_{\spl(W_2)}$ and $\lp G_{\spl(W_2)} \rp_{\spl(W_1)}$, as well as $G_{\spl(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$, are CDMGs, and the asserted equality is an equality of CDMGs.

rows.title: DisjointHardInterventions
NodeSplittingHard
rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Node-splitting hard intervention on CADMGs]
  \label{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CADMG and $W \ins V$ a subset of the output nodes.
    The \emph{single-world intervention graph (SWIG)} w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CADMG: 
    \[ G_{\swig(W)} :=\lp J_{\swig(W)}, V_{\swig(W)}, E_{\swig(W)},L_{\swig(W)} \rp,\]
    %\[ G_{\swig(W)} := G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    %\[G((V\sm W) \dcup W^o|\doit(W^i \dcup J)):=G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    constructed as follows.
    We first make two disjont copies of the nodes in $W$:
    \[ W^o:=\lC w^o\st w \in W \rC, \qquad W^i:=\lC w^i \st w \in W \rC.  \]
    Note that we consider $w^o \neq w^i$ for $w \in W$.
  However, for brevity, for $v \in J \cup V \sm W$ we put:
  \[ v^o:=v^i:=v.  \]
  We then define:
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
      \item $J_{\swig(W)} := J \dcup W^i$,
      \item $V_{\swig(W)} := (V \sm W) \dcup W^o$, %which could be identified with $V$ again if we want to make the identification $W=W^o$.
      \item $E_{\swig(W)} := \lC v^i_1 \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E  \rC$,
      \item $L_{\swig(W)} :=\lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$.
  \end{enumerate}
  where we turn all nodes of $W^i$ into input nodes, removing all edges into $W^i$,
  and we turn all nodes of $W^o$ into output nodes, removing all edges out of $W^o$.
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}

rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Extending CDMGs with intervention nodes]\label{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}
  Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes.
  The \emph{extended CDMG} of $G$ w.r.t.\ nodes $W \ins J \cup V$  and corresponding \emph{intervention nodes} 
%  $I_W = \{I_w \,|\, w \in W\}$ \Joris{with $I_j:=j$ for $j \in J$},
  $I_W = \{I_w \,|\, w \in W\}$ with $I_j:=j$ for $j \in J \cap W$,
  is the CDMG: 
  \[ G_{\doit(I_W)}:=(J_{\doit(I_W)},V_{\doit(I_W)},E_{\doit(I_W)},L_{\doit(I_W)}),\]
  %\[G(V|\doit(J \dcup I_W)):=G_{\doit(I_W)}=(J_{\doit(I_W)},V_{\doit(I_W)},E_{\doit(I_W)},L_{\doit(I_W)}),\]
  where:
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
%      \item $J_{\doit(I_W)}:= J\, \dot{\cup} \, \lC I_w\,|\, w \in W \Joris{\sm J} \rC$, %not W \sm J, makes weak and hard interventions commute
      \item $J_{\doit(I_W)}:= J\, \dot{\cup} \, \lC I_w\,|\, w \in W \sm J \rC$, %not W \sm J, makes weak and hard interventions commute
      \item $V_{\doit(I_W)}:= V$,
      \item $E_{\doit(I_W)}:= E\, \dot{\cup} \, \lC I_w \tuh w \,|\, w \in W\sm J \rC$,
      \item $L_{\doit(I_W)}:= L$,
  \end{enumerate}
%  where we just add \Joris{nodes $I_w$ for $w \in W \sm J$ and} edges $I_w \tuh w$ for $ w \in W \sm J$. 
  where we just add nodes $I_w$ for $w \in W \sm J$ and edges $I_w \tuh w$ for $ w \in W \sm J$.
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}

rows.tex_block:
\begin{claimmark}
\begin{Rem}
    If a CDMG $G$ is acyclic then all non-colliders are blockable.
    So, the partial condition for $i\sigma$-separation
    ``a blockable non-collider in $C$''
    can be simplified to ``(any) non-collider in $C$''.
    
    So in the acyclic case we can simplify the notion of $i\sigma$-separation, 
    which we will refer to as \emph{$id$-separation}.
    However, in the non-acyclic setting $id$-separation (``(any) non-collider in $C$'') and $i\sigma$-separation (``a blockable non-collider in $C$'') are clearly not equivalent.

    It turned out that in the non-acyclic case $i\sigma$-separation is the more general concept (and as said above it also captures the acyclic case equivalently well), see \cite{FM17,FM18,FM20,BFPM21}.
    We will first focus on CADMGs (acyclic) for which we can restrict ourselves to the somewhat simpler $id$-separation.
    Later, we will pick up $i\sigma$-separation again when we deal with cycles.
\end{Rem}
\end{claimmark}

rows.title: LabelRoman
rows.tex_block:
\begin{claimmark}
\begin{Lem}\label{lem:replace_walk}
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $C \subseteq V \cup J$ and $\pi = \lp  v_0 \sus \cdots \sus v_n \rp$ be a $C$-$\sigma$-open walk in $G$.
  Suppose $v_i \in \Sc^G(v_j)$ for some $i,j \in \{0,\dots,n\}$ with $i < j$.
If we then replace the subwalk $v_i \sus \cdots \sus v_j$ of $\pi$ by 
  \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
      \item a shortest directed path $v_i \tuh \cdots \tuh v_j$ in $G$ if $j = n$ or if $v_j \tuh v_{j+1}$ on $\pi$, or 
    \item a shortest directed path $v_i \hut \cdots \hut v_j$ in $G$ otherwise,
  \end{enumerate}
  then this new subwalk is entirely within $\Sc^G(v_j)$ and the modified walk $\pi'$ is still $C$-$\sigma$-open.
\begin{proof}
$\pi'$ cannot become $C$-$\sigma$-blocked at one of the initial nodes $v_0, \dots, v_{i-1}$ or at one of the final nodes $v_{j+1}, \dots, v_n$ on $\pi'$, since these nodes occur in the same local configuration on $\pi$ and are not $C$-$\sigma$-blocked on $\pi$ by assumption.
Furthermore, $\pi'$ cannot become $C$-$\sigma$-blocked at one of the nodes strictly between $v_i$ and $v_j$ on $\pi'$ (if there are any), since these nodes are all non-endnode non-colliders that only point to nodes in the same strongly connected component $\Sc^G(v_j)$. It is also worth noting that $\pi'$ cannot become $C$-$\sigma$-blocked at any of its endnodes, which could be $v_i$ or $v_j$ or both, because those are the same in $\pi$. So in the following we can w.l.o.g.\ assume that both $v_i$ and $v_j$ are non-endnodes of $\pi$ and thus $\pi'$.

Case (i). By assumption $v_j$ is either a fork or a right chain (or the right endnode) on $\pi$ that is $C$-$\sigma$-open. Since the same blocking criteria apply to $v_j$ on $\pi'$ it remains $C$-$\sigma$-open on $\pi'$. If $v_i=v_j$ then also $v_i$ is $C$-$\sigma$-open on $\pi'$ (if $v_i$ is the left endnode or not). If $v_i \neq v_j$, then the new directed path $v_i \tuh \cdots \tuh v_j$ in $\pi'$ is $C$-$\sigma$-open at $v_i$ because all nodes in between lie in the same stronly connected component $\Sc^G(v_i)$ (or $v_i$ is the left endnode anyways).

 Case (ii). Since case (i) is solved we can assume that we have $j<n$ with $v_j \hus v_{j+1}$ in $\pi$. 
 If $v_{i-1} \hut v_i$ on $\pi'$ (or $v_i$ the left endnode) then this case is analogous to case (i). 
 So we can also assume that we have $i > 0$ and $v_{i-1} \suh v_i$ on $\pi$. So $\pi$ looks as follows:
 \[\pi:\qquad \cdots v_{i-1} \suh v_i \sus \cdots \sus v_j \hus v_{j+1} \cdots. \]
 So there must be a smallest number $k \in \lC i,\dots,j\rC$ such that a collider appears at $v_k$ on $\pi$:
 \[\pi:\qquad \cdots v_{i-1} \suh v_i \tuh \cdots \tuh v_k \hus \cdots \sus v_j \hus v_{j+1} \cdots. \]
 Since $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-open we have $v_k \in \Anc^G(C)$. Since $v_i \in \Anc^G(v_k)$ (otherwise $v_k$ would not be the first collider appearing after $v_i$) we thus have that also $v_i \in \Anc^G(C)$.
 So if we replace the subwalk $v_i \sus \cdots \sus v_j$ of $\pi$ by the shortest directed path $v_i \hut \cdots \hut v_j$ in $G$ we then get for $\pi'$ the following situation:
 \[\pi':\qquad \cdots v_{i-1} \suh v_i \hut \cdots \hut  v_j \hus v_{j+1} \cdots, \]
 which is then $C$-$\sigma$-open at $v_i$ as $v_i \in \Anc^G(C)$. Note that this holds also when $v_i=v_j$.
 If $v_i \neq v_j$ then $v_j$ is also $C$-$\sigma$-open on $\pi'$ as $v_j$ points left to a node in the same strongly connected component as $v_j$. 

 So in all cases $\pi'$ stays $C$-$\sigma$-open.
\end{proof}
\end{Lem}
\end{claimmark}

TheStandardAcyclification
rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Eg}[The standard acyclification]
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG. Then we define the \emph{standard acyclification} of $G$ as the CDMG
    $ G'=(J,V,E',L')$
    where:
      \begin{align*} 
          E' := & \lC v_1 \tuh v_2  \st v_1 \in J \cup V, v_2 \in V, v_2 \notin \Sc^G(v_1),\exists v_2' \in \Sc^G(v_2):\, 
   v_1 \tuh v_2' \in E\rC,\\
          L' := &\lC v_1 \huh v_2  \st v_1,v_2 \in V, v_1 \neq v_2, \exists v_1' \in \Sc^G(v_1), v_2' \in \Sc^G(v_2):\, v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC \\ \cup &
\lC v_1 \huh v_2  \st v_1,v_2 \in V, v_1 \neq v_2, v_1 \in \Sc^G(v_2) \rC.
\end{align*}
% claim_3_32
\begin{claimmark}
The standard acyclification of a CDMG is acyclic, i.e.\ a CADMG.
\end{claimmark}
\begin{proof}
    Assume that $G'$ is not acyclic. Then there exists a non-trivial cyclic directed walk in $G'$:
    \[ v_1 \tuh v_2 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_k \tuh v_1, \]
    for some $k \ge 1$. It is clear that $k \ge 2$ because clearly $v_1 \in \Sc^G(v_1)$, which rules out the existence of an edge $v_1 \tuh v_1 \in G'$. 
    For simplicity we now identify $v_{k+1} := v_1$ in the following.
    By construction of $G'$ for every $i=1,\dots,k$ there exists $v'_{i+1} \in \Sc^G(v_{i+1})$ 
    such that the edge $v_i \tuh v_{i+1}'$ exists in $G$. Since $v'_{i+1} \in \Sc^G(v_{i+1})$ there also exists a directed walk in $G$: 
     \[v'_{i+1} \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{i+1}.\]
     Concatenating all directed walks we get the cyclic directed walk in $G$:
    \[ v_1 \tuh v_2' \tuh \cdots \tuh v_2 \tuh v_3' \tuh \cdots \tuh v_k 
    \tuh v_1' \tuh \cdots \tuh v_1. \]
    This shows that $v_2 \in \Sc^G(v_1)$, which is a contradiction to the existence of the edge $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G'$.
    So a non-trivial cyclic directed walk in $G'$ cannot exist in the first place. So $G'$ must be acyclic.
\end{proof}
\end{Eg}
\end{defmark}

rows.title: AcyclificationIsAcyclic
AsymmetricSeparoidAxioms
rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
% claim_3_33
\begin{claimmark}
\begin{DefThm}[(Asymmetric) separoid axioms for $i\sigma$-separation/$id$-separation]
    \label{d-sep-separoid-axioms}
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $A,B,C,D \ins J \cup V$ subsets of nodes.
Then the ternary relations $\iPerp = \idPerp_G$ and $\iPerp = \isPerp_G$ satisfy the following rules:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\alph*)]
    \item Extended Left Redundancy: 
    \item[] $D \ins A \implies D \iPerp  B \given A$.
    \item $J$-Restricted Right Redundancy: 
    \item[] $A \iPerp  \emptyset \given C \cup J$ always holds.
    \item $J$-Inverted Right Decomposition: 
    \item[] $A \iPerp  B \given C \implies A \iPerp  J \cup B \given C$.
	\item Left Decomposition:  
	\item[]$A \cup D \iPerp  B \given C \implies D \iPerp  B \given C$.
  \item Right Decomposition:  	
    \item[] $A \iPerp  B \cup D\given C \implies A \iPerp  D \given C$.
    \item Left Weak Union:
    \item[] $A \cup D \iPerp  B  \given C \implies A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C$.
  \item Right Weak Union:\item[] $A \iPerp  B \cup D \given C \implies A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C$.
\item Left Contraction:\item[] 
 $ (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \land (D \iPerp  B \given C) \implies A \cup D \iPerp  B \given C$.
\item Right Contraction:
\item[] $ (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \land (A \iPerp  D \given C)  \implies A \iPerp  B \cup D \given C$.
\item Right Cross Contraction:
\item[] $ (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \land (D \iPerp  A \given C) \implies A  \iPerp  B \cup D \given C$.
\item Flipped Left Cross Contraction: 
\item[] $ (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \land (B \iPerp  D \given C) \implies B \iPerp  A \cup D \given C$.
\end{enumerate}
In particular, we have the equivalences:
$$ (A \iPerp  B \cup D \given C) \quad\iff\quad (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \quad\land\quad (A \iPerp  D \given C)  ,$$
%and:
$$ (A \cup D \iPerp  B \given C) \quad\iff\quad (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \quad\land\quad (D \iPerp  B \given C).$$
%Finally, if $J = \emptyset$ then 
We also get:
\begin{enumerate}[resume,label=\alph*)]
    \item $J$-Restricted Symmetry: 
	\item[] $A \iPerp  B \given C \cup J \implies B \iPerp A \given C \cup J$. 
\end{enumerate}
For the special case of $J=\emptyset$ we have thus (unrestricted) Symmetry.
\end{DefThm}
\end{claimmark}
\end{defmark}

rows.title: AsymmetricSeparoidAxioms
rows.tex_block:
\begin{claimmark}
\begin{DefThm}[(Asymmetric) separoid axioms for $i\sigma$-separation/$id$-separation]
    \label{d-sep-separoid-axioms}
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $A,B,C,D \ins J \cup V$ subsets of nodes.
Then the ternary relations $\iPerp = \idPerp_G$ and $\iPerp = \isPerp_G$ satisfy the following rules:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\alph*)]
    \item Extended Left Redundancy: 
    \item[] $D \ins A \implies D \iPerp  B \given A$.
    \item $J$-Restricted Right Redundancy: 
    \item[] $A \iPerp  \emptyset \given C \cup J$ always holds.
    \item $J$-Inverted Right Decomposition: 
    \item[] $A \iPerp  B \given C \implies A \iPerp  J \cup B \given C$.
	\item Left Decomposition:  
	\item[]$A \cup D \iPerp  B \given C \implies D \iPerp  B \given C$.
  \item Right Decomposition:  	
    \item[] $A \iPerp  B \cup D\given C \implies A \iPerp  D \given C$.
    \item Left Weak Union:
    \item[] $A \cup D \iPerp  B  \given C \implies A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C$.
  \item Right Weak Union:\item[] $A \iPerp  B \cup D \given C \implies A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C$.
\item Left Contraction:\item[] 
 $ (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \land (D \iPerp  B \given C) \implies A \cup D \iPerp  B \given C$.
\item Right Contraction:
\item[] $ (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \land (A \iPerp  D \given C)  \implies A \iPerp  B \cup D \given C$.
\item Right Cross Contraction:
\item[] $ (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \land (D \iPerp  A \given C) \implies A  \iPerp  B \cup D \given C$.
\item Flipped Left Cross Contraction: 
\item[] $ (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \land (B \iPerp  D \given C) \implies B \iPerp  A \cup D \given C$.
\end{enumerate}
In particular, we have the equivalences:
$$ (A \iPerp  B \cup D \given C) \quad\iff\quad (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \quad\land\quad (A \iPerp  D \given C)  ,$$
%and:
$$ (A \cup D \iPerp  B \given C) \quad\iff\quad (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \quad\land\quad (D \iPerp  B \given C).$$
%Finally, if $J = \emptyset$ then 
We also get:
\begin{enumerate}[resume,label=\alph*)]
    \item $J$-Restricted Symmetry: 
	\item[] $A \iPerp  B \given C \cup J \implies B \iPerp A \given C \cup J$. 
\end{enumerate}
For the special case of $J=\emptyset$ we have thus (unrestricted) Symmetry.
\end{DefThm}
\end{claimmark}
